title: Shop Squared
tileSummary: Inventory management app for ecommerce and event workflows.

text.title: Summary
paragraphs: A browser-based inventory management app built with Vue 3 and Vite, designed initially for our Spring Con inventory. It combines a fast, clean frontend with a Node-based architecture and a Supabase backend to manage bins, items, and product variations efficiently.

The app includes a login system with session persistence, uses GitHub secrets to protect sensitive configuration, and processes CSV/XLSX files entirely in the browser for secure, client-side data handling, making it a practical tool for real-time inventory tracking in ecommerce and event environments.

title: Zoom Bot
tileSummary: A bot that integrates with OBS Studio in Zoom meetings to trigger automated responses, visuals, and sound effects.

text.title: Summary
paragraphs: The Zoom Bot is a Node.js application that connects to Zoom meetings using the Zoom API and integrates with OBS Studio to create interactive meeting experiences.

It listens for specific triggers in the meeting, such as chat messages or participant actions, and responds by activating predefined scenes, visuals, or sound effects in OBS. This allows for dynamic presentations, engaging webinars, and enhanced virtual events by automating responses based on real-time meeting interactions.

text.title: Summary
paragraphs: Ninja Blog is a personal project showcasing various web development skills and projects. It serves as a portfolio and a platform to share insights, tutorials, and updates on ongoing projects.

title: Ninja Internal Demos
tileSummary: Internal index of real estate lead generation demos, organized by team member.

text.title: Summary
paragraphs: A central hub for the HomeValue Digital Network's real estate lead generation ecosystem, built as a lightweight static site and deployed via GitHub Pages.

It indexes live demo implementations across ten lead-gen verticals — military, pre-approval, FSBO, title verification, investor leads, agent matching, direct home purchase, home valuation for sellers, IDX search and alerts, and text-based lead capture — with each category linked to working pages from team members Nik, Juan, and Ryan.
The site is intended for internal review and demonstration only, giving the team a single jumping-off point to compare implementations side by side.

title: Direct Mail Sphere
tileSummary: A full-stack platform for real estate agents to create targeted direct mail campaigns.

text.title: Summary
paragraphs: Direct Mail Sphere is a full-stack web application built with Vue 3 (Vite) on the frontend and Node.js/Express on the backend, designed as a portal for real estate agents to create and manage targeted direct mail campaigns.

It features both a client-facing interface for building campaigns and an admin portal for managing and tracking orders, along with secure authentication, email verification, and email integration via Resend.
The system generates structured CSV files that integrate directly with FusionPro, enabling automated PDF creation and streamlining the workflow from campaign setup to print production and delivery.

title: Lofty Forms
tileSummary: Dynamic onboarding forms with an admin panel, per-office QR codes, and live Lofty API integration.

text.title: Summary
paragraphs: A dynamic form application built to streamline office onboarding workflows. The admin panel pulls offices in real time from the Lofty API through a Node.js/Express proxy, lets an admin toggle which social and link fields appear, and generates a unique form URL plus a downloadable QR code for each office.

The form itself is a Vue 3 (Vite) single-page app driven by a 27-field config file, with per-field validation for names, emails, phone numbers, URLs, and integers, real-time format hints, and an office field that auto-fills from the URL slug. Submissions come back as a CSV named after the office and submitter.

Credentials live in a backend .env file so the Lofty API key never reaches the browser. Planned next steps include Supabase-backed submission storage, role-based auth for admins and team leaders, and caching of office data to reduce API calls.

title: BuyMyHouse
tileSummary: A platform that captures motivated sellers, connects them with buyers or specialists, and offers sale paths based on their needs.

text.title: Summary
paragraphs: The purpose of this platform is to provide homeowners with a flexible, modern selling experience that reflects today’s real estate market, where cash offers and traditional listings coexist. As the space has evolved beyond early players like Opendoor and Offerpad, sellers now expect multiple paths when considering a sale.

This platform acts as a neutral, intent-driven hub that captures motivated sellers, connects them with a potential buyer or specialist, and presents tailored options—whether that’s evaluating competitive cash offers from a network of investors or pursuing a full market listing.

By combining streamlined technology with real human expertise, it creates a seamless bridge between alternative selling methods and traditional real estate, giving sellers the clarity and control to choose the best path forward.

text.title: Challenges
paragraphs: Early versions of the platform face challenges in delivering core functionality such as a multi-step seller intake form, reliable email routing to cash buyers, basic CRM tracking, and a simple admin dashboard to manage submissions.

Beyond development, building a strong investor network and handling sensitive seller data introduce business and privacy hurdles, while also requiring an agent-matching fallback when cash offers don’t meet seller expectations.
Additionally, the MVP has little technical moat and risks being over-engineered, making execution, partnerships, and user trust the biggest challenges in the initial phase.

title: Find My Agent
tileSummary: A platform that matches homeowners with the most qualified real estate agent for their specific situation.

text.title: Summary
paragraphs: This platform is designed to eliminate the guesswork in choosing a real estate agent by connecting homeowners with specialists who have proven experience in specific scenarios such as VA loans, probate, divorce, and investment properties.
Instead of presenting users with a broad directory, it uses a structured intake process and data-driven matching system to identify the single most qualified agent for each unique situation.

By focusing on precision over volume, the platform ensures that users are paired with professionals who understand the nuances of their needs, delivering a more efficient, personalized, and high-confidence real estate experience.

text.title: Challenges
paragraphs: Early versions of the platform face challenges in building a reliable and complete agent dataset with verified specialization and performance metrics to support accurate matching.
Designing a matching system that feels truly personalized—without overcomplicating the logic—is also a key hurdle, especially at the MVP stage.
Additionally, establishing trust in the "single match" approach and ensuring consistent communication between users and agents are critical to adoption and long-term success.

title: Text-Based Lead Capture
tileSummary: A text-based real estate platform that captures buyer intent by instantly delivering property details via SMS and connecting them with agents.

text.title: Summary
paragraphs: This platform is designed to bridge offline and online real estate engagement by allowing users to instantly access property information through a simple text message. By texting a memorable short code, users can receive real-time details such as pricing, photos, and property specs without needing apps or logins.
The system captures high-intent buyers at the exact moment of interest—whether they’re viewing a yard sign, billboard, or advertisement—and routes inquiries directly to local agents for follow-up.

By combining speed, simplicity, and direct agent connection, the platform transforms passive interest into immediate, actionable leads while creating a seamless mobile-first property discovery experience.

text.title: Challenges
paragraphs: Early versions of the platform face challenges around setting up reliable SMS infrastructure, including short code registration, Twilio integration, and strict TCPA compliance for messaging.
Building accurate keyword-based routing and ensuring fast, consistent delivery of property data—especially without full MLS integration—are also key hurdles at the MVP stage.
Additionally, coordinating agent routing, avoiding keyword conflicts, and establishing a scalable system for notifications and lead handling are critical for long-term success.

title: Military Leads
tileSummary: A lead-matching platform that pairs active-duty members, veterans, and military families with real estate agents who specialize in VA loans and PCS moves.

text.title: Summary
paragraphs: Military Leads is a consumer-facing lead page for the HomeValue Digital Network's military vertical. It connects active-duty members, veterans, reservists, and military spouses with a single verified real estate agent who has demonstrated experience in VA-backed transactions, PCS relocations, and military-adjacent scenarios like BAH-driven buying decisions.

The intake form captures military status, transaction need (buying, selling, PCS, refinance), target location, and contact info, and promises a specialist match within 48 hours rather than returning a list of generalists to sift through.

Alongside the matching flow, the page includes two interactive utilities: a VA loan payment calculator (home price, loan term, rate, funding fee tier) and a BAH lookup based on 2025 DoD data keyed by pay grade, dependency status, and duty station.

text.title: Challenges
paragraphs: The "verified specialist" promise is only as strong as the data behind it. Without access to MLS transaction history or a curated agent roster, "VA-certified" reduces to self-reported badges — exactly the problem the platform is positioned to solve against competitors like Zillow Agent Finder or HomeLight.

Single-match routing concentrates risk. One slow response, one bad handoff, or one mismatch erodes trust in a way that a ten-agent list does not, so the backend pipeline has to be operationally tight before scale.

Military-adjacent reference data decays. BAH rates update annually and VA funding fee tiers can shift with policy — any hard-coded 2025 values need a refresh path (live feed, scheduled update, or admin UI) or the calculators will quietly become wrong.

text.title: Needed Tech
paragraphs: A VA-specialized agent database with verified transaction history — sourced from Aperture Network intelligence, MLS integrations, or manual curation at MVP — so specialization is provable rather than claimed.

A form submission endpoint and agent-routing layer: a lightweight backend (Netlify Functions, Formspree, or a small Node/Express service) plus a CRM for intake, assignment, and SLA tracking against the 48-hour promise.
A refresh path for BAH and VA loan parameters so calculator outputs stay accurate as DoD rates and funding fees update year over year.

text.title: Current State
paragraphs: Deployed as a static site on GitHub Pages — HTML with Tailwind CSS loaded via CDN and Google Fonts (Epilogue, Manrope), no build step required.

The intake form, VA loan calculator, and BAH lookup are all wired up and interactive on the frontend. Form submissions, agent roster, and matching pipeline are not yet connected — the page is currently a demo of the consumer experience ahead of the backend and data work required to actually fulfill matches.

title: PreApprove
tileSummary: A mortgage preapproval matching service that routes borrowers into the right loan program and hands them to 2–3 vetted lenders — no hard credit pull.

text.title: Summary
paragraphs: PreApprove is a consumer-facing preapproval front door for the HomeValue Digital Network. Rather than acting as a single lender, it positions as a neutral qualification and comparison tool that matches each borrower into the right loan program — VA, FHA, USDA, Conventional, or Jumbo — and hands them off to two to three vetted lenders who specialize in that program.

The interaction is a three-step wizard. Step one assesses situation (veteran status, first-time buyer, rural/suburban property, credit strength, target price band). Step two collects details (estimated purchase price, timeline, property type, employment status, credit score range). Step three confirms the match and sets the expectation that a lending specialist will reach out within one business day.

Within the ecosystem, PreApprove is the gateway that converts browsers into qualified buyers. It feeds preapproved consumers into agent matching with verified readiness, integrates naturally with the military vertical (VA) and first-time buyer pathways (FHA), and gives the IDX and agent-matching layers a reliable signal for who is actually transaction-ready.

text.title: Challenges
paragraphs: Competing against category giants — Rocket Mortgage, Better, LoanDepot, LendingTree — the "2–3 vetted lenders per program" promise is only credible if the lender roster is real, curated, and held accountable to the one-business-day SLA. Recruiting and managing those partnerships is the hard part, not the UI.

"Free · No Hard Credit Pull" is both a trust message and a technical constraint. Honoring it at scale pushes toward soft-pull APIs (Plaid, Experian, Equifax) which add integration cost and compliance surface, and until a soft pull runs, the matching engine is operating on self-reported data.

Loan program guidelines drift. Conforming loan limits, FHA county ceilings, VA funding fee tiers, and USDA eligibility maps all change — any hard-coded values in the wizard or FAQ erode trust the moment a borrower cross-checks against Bankrate or a lender site.

text.title: Needed Tech
paragraphs: A lender network and routing engine: two to three vetted lending partners per program, plus rules that map borrower inputs (credit band, loan size, geography, program eligibility) to the right partner. Without the roster, matching is a UI shell.

Form submission backend and CRM: a live endpoint (Netlify Functions, Formspree, or a light Node/Express service), a submission store, and CRM wiring so lender follow-up is tracked against the one-business-day promise rather than relying on manual handoff.

Soft-pull credit integration (Plaid, Experian, or Equifax consumer APIs) if the "no hard credit pull" positioning graduates from messaging into an actual prequalification score. A live feed for conforming limits, FHA ceilings, and VA funding fees keeps the wizard and FAQ accurate year over year.

Rate alert and nurture infrastructure: email or SMS triggers tied to rate movement keep long-timeline buyers engaged between first visit and close — the PDF calls this out as a key ecosystem contribution.

text.title: Current State
paragraphs: Deployed as a static site on GitHub Pages — a single index.html, no frameworks, no build step. The entire three-step wizard runs client-side and the FAQ is fleshed out with substantive answers on preapproval vs. prequalification, credit-pull behavior, and the matching methodology.

The consumer flow and content layer are effectively complete. What isn't wired: the lender roster, the form submission endpoint, the routing logic that would turn a submitted form into an actual lender handoff, and any credit-data integration. The page currently demonstrates the intended experience ahead of the partnership and backend work needed to fulfill matches.

title: FSBO Support
tileSummary: A support-oriented toolkit and education site for homeowners selling independently — pricing, listing, and offer-handling guidance without the agent-commission pitch.

text.title: Summary
paragraphs: FSBO Support is the For Sale By Owner vertical of the HomeValue Digital Network, built as a value-first toolkit for the roughly 7–10% of home sales that start without an agent. Rather than pitching an agent upfront, it positions as free support — pricing help, listing templates, and offer-handling guidance — with the promise that a seller can get listing-ready in about 30 minutes.

The experience is structured around the three stages of a FSBO sale: price it right (using a valuation tool backed by recent local sales), list it properly (photography checklist, listing description template, platform guidance), and handle offers yourself (offer review and negotiation guide). A home valuation form collects address, name, email, optional phone, and current stage (thinking, planning, listed, struggling), and confirms that a valuation summary is on the way.

Within the ecosystem, FSBO Support captures independent-minded sellers who resist traditional agent outreach and builds the trust needed for later conversion. When the DIY path stalls — stale listing, offer fell through, inspection surprise — the stage field on the form becomes the natural trigger for handing the seller into agent matching or the cash offer vertical.

text.title: Challenges
paragraphs: FSBO sellers are explicitly DIY-minded and allergic to conversion pressure. The "support over sales" positioning has to be real — if the tools are thin or the downloads are gated behind aggressive lead capture, the page becomes indistinguishable from adversarial competitors like FSBO.com, ForSaleByOwner.com, or the Zillow FSBO surface.

Pricing accuracy is the central promise. "Analyze recent local sales data" requires an AVM or comparable-sales feed behind the valuation form; without it, the tool is a lead-capture form with a promise attached, and a pricing miss is the single fastest way to lose seller trust.

FSBO sales have a predictable frustration point — most FSBO attempts end with an agent or a sale below market. Timing the pivot to agent matching or a cash offer without feeling opportunistic is a CRM and messaging problem, not a content one, and it's where the ecosystem handoff either works or feels like a bait-and-switch.

text.title: Needed Tech
paragraphs: An AVM or comparable-sales data source (ATTOM, HouseCanary, or direct MLS integration) to back the valuation form's "recent local sales" claim — and eventually power the pricing checklist with specific numbers rather than generic guidance.

Actual downloadable assets and a delivery path. The Pricing Checklist, Listing Description Template, and Offer Review Guide are referenced today but the links read as placeholders. Email-gated delivery is nurture-friendly; direct download is friction-free. The trade-off is worth picking deliberately.

Form submission backend and CRM with stage-aware triggers: the form already captures where the seller is in the journey (thinking / planning / listed / struggling). Storing that and triggering follow-up when the seller self-reports "struggling" is the mechanism that turns FSBO Support into a live pipeline feeder for agent matching and cash offers.

Long-tail SEO content depth. The PDF identifies "how to" content as the FSBO vertical's SEO advantage — each stage (pricing, photography, MLS posting, contract-to-close) should eventually exist as a real article, not a section header.

text.title: Current State
paragraphs: Deployed as a static site on GitHub Pages — a single index.html, no frameworks, no build step, no backend in the repo.

The home valuation form and the three-step content frame (price → list → handle offers) are in place, and the valuation submission shows a success confirmation promising a summary. The downloadable resources and the "Know When to Call an Agent" criteria are present as content references rather than fully developed assets, and the valuation form doesn't yet have a visible backend, AVM feed, or CRM destination wired up.

Current build is a polished front door for the FSBO journey ahead of the data sources, asset production, and backend plumbing needed to fulfill on the toolkit promise and feed the downstream ecosystem.
